coredata.dc:title: Chapter 12 Quantum dots A novel fluorescent probe for bioimaging and drug delivery applications
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2018 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ilaiyaraja, Nallamuthu
Fathima, Syeda J.
Khanum, Farhath
coredata.dc:description: Quantum dots (QDs) are novel class of fluorescent probe with nanometer dimensions. They exhibit excellent photoelectronic properties, including broad excitation spectra, tunable fluorescence, and multicolor fluorescence that are lacking in the traditional organic fluorescents. QS have wider applications in biosciences, including bioimaging, drug delivery and bimolecular nanosensors; they can be biofunctionalized for targeted fluorescence imaging of cells and organs. Recent developments have led to synthesis of low toxic QDs for in vivo applications. However, further research is needed to produce more biocompatibility and nontoxic nanoparticles using efficient and low-cost methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Light-emitting nanocrystals
bioconjugation
targeted fluorescence imaging
safety aspects

Mercaptophenylboronic acid-capped Mn-coated ZnS quantum dots (QDs@MBA)
Graphene oxide (NH2-GO) composited with CdS quantum dots (QDs)
l-Cysteine capped Mn coated ZnS quantum dots (l-cys ZnS:Mn QDs)

chapter.ce:label: Chapter 12
chapter.ce:title: Quantum dots
chapter.ce:subtitle: A novel fluorescent probe for bioimaging and drug delivery applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Defence Food Research Laboratory (DFRL), Mysore, Karnataka, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantum dots (QDs) are novel class of fluorescent probe with nanometer dimensions. They exhibit excellent photoelectronic properties, including broad excitation spectra, tunable fluorescence, and multicolor fluorescence that are lacking in the traditional organic fluorescents. QS have wider applications in biosciences, including bioimaging, drug delivery and bimolecular nanosensors; they can be biofunctionalized for targeted fluorescence imaging of cells and organs. Recent developments have led to synthesis of low toxic QDs for in vivo applications. However, further research is needed to produce more biocompatibility and nontoxic nanoparticles using efficient and low-cost methods.

Types of Quantum Dots 534
12.2.1

12.5.1

12.5.3

12.5.4
Application of Quantum Dots in Biopharmaceuticals 540

Quantum Dot–Based Nanobiosensors 545
12.8.1
Glucose Sensors 546
12.8.2

12.8.3
Detection of Vitamins 550
12.8.4

Detection of Metal Ions 551
12.8.8
Detection of Pathogens 552

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology is one of the rapidly evolving fields in many areas of science. Over the last few decades, medical investigations, particularly biological imaging and biomedical diagnostic applications, have been revolutionized with the advent of quantum dot (QD)-based optical techniques. In this regard, we have summarized here the basic properties, synthesis methods, and biological applications of QDs. The toxicological aspects of these nanoparticles have been covered at the end of the chapter.

QDs are nanocrystals made of inorganic semiconductors, ranging in size from 1 to 10nm and they are a novel class of light-emitting particles or fluorescent probes. They are small enough to exhibit three-dimensional (3D) quantum confinements responsible for the unique optical properties, i.e., tuning of absorbance and emission with sizes known as quantum confinement. The size of the particle is most important in determining the color of the emitted light. The emission spectrum shifts from blue to red color as the size of the particle becomes larger. QDs are composed of elements from groups of II–VI (CdSe, CdTe), III–V (InP, InAs), and IV–VI materials from the periodic tables, as shown in Fig. 12.1 .

Semiconductors have electrical properties in between the conductors and insulators. They contain a bandgap that separates the valence band from conduction band (Fig. 12.2 ). The latter contains valance electrons that are residing in the outer most shell of an atom with the highest energies. In solid formations, these electrons are mostly affected. The next higher energy band is called conduction or empty band and also referred to as the lowest unfilled energy band. The gap between these two bands is called a bandgap. When a valence electron absorbs light with sufficient photon energy/thermal energy it can cross the energy gap and enter into conduction bands. This leaves a hole in the valance band; as a result an electron–hole pair is produced, known as excitons. Electrons can only move freely in a conduction band. When the electron–hole pairs recombine, the energy is emitted as fluorescence. It can happen either by radiative recombination, where photons are emitted, or by nonradiative recombinations, where phonons (lattice vibrations) are emitted (True and Gao, 2007). Electrons in atoms have the ΔE in the range of 1–10eV, L=0.1nm, whereas an electron–hole pair (excitons) in semiconductor QDs have ΔE 0.1eV, L≈10nm.

In a semiconductor, when an electron is excited from the valence band to conduction band, it leaves a hole behind it. As a consequence, a physical distance is created between the promoted electron and hole. This hole can take an electron from the adjacent atom and thereby a positive hole can move within a valance band from one atom to another. The tuning of bandgap can happen only in a certain size range by varying the size of the particles; beyond a certain critical size, QDs lose their optical properties and resemble that of bulk particles. Upon excitation with a light source, varying particle sizes of QDs emit multicolor-fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 12.3 .

Quantum dots (QDs) are an excellent alternative to conventional organic fluorophores. The latter group includes fluorescein, green multicolor-fluorescent proteins, rhodamine, or chemically synthesized fluorescent dyes (FAM, FITC, Cy3/Cy5 and Texas red) and has been extensively used for tagging biological molecules. However, they also suffer from disadvantages of showing nonfluoresce continuously for extended periods of time, pH sensitivity, blinking, and undergoing fast degradation or photo-bleach, and therefore not amenable for multicolor applications. Moreover, the broad spectra of excitation and emission can even cause cross-talks, as in case of fluorescent proteins.

The fluorescence of the QDs can be directly measured in biological materials using fluorescent microscopy. Currently, extensive research is being carried out worldwide to develop multifunctional QDs with advanced physiochemical properties for their increased stability under complex in vitro and in vivo conditions.
The QDs can be classified as follows:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simple or Core Type Quantum Dots
ce:section.ce:para: These QDs are made from a single material with uniform internal composition, such as chalcogenides (selenide, sulfide, and telluride) of cadmium, zinc, and lead, etc. This type includes core types of CdTe and PbS. By simply manipulating the crystalline size of the materials, the photoelectric properties can be tuned.

The fluorescence QY of QDs is reduced when recombination of electron–hole pairs or exciton decay occurs through nonradiative pathways other than the radiative modes. To overcome this problem, shells of another higher bandgap semiconducting material can be encapsulated around them. Such a combination of small bandgap materials embedded in larger wider bandgap materials are known as core–shell quantum dots (CSQDs). For example, coating of ZnS (shell) over CdSe (core) improves the QY by passivizing nonradiative recombination sites (Fig. 12.4 ). By this approach, the photophysical properties of QDs can be effectively modified.

Tuning the properties can also be achieved by alloying two semiconductor QDs together with both homogeneous and gradient internal structures. Here, the size is unaltered and they are formed by compositing varying bandgap semiconductors. For example, an alloyed 6nm diameter QD of CdSeS/ZnS emits different wavelengths of light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods of Synthesis of Quantum Dots
ce:section.ce:para: QDs can be synthesized by different routes/methods. Some methods involve time consuming processes, expensive materials, or use very extreme reaction conditions. Whereas, others are simple, reproducible, cost effective, and so on. Researchers have investigated numerous strategies to fabricate perfect QDs with multipurpose uses. Here, we discuss in brief the synthesis, solubilization, and biocoagulation aspects of the QDS.

In most biological applications, the colloidal forms of QD are widely used. Generally, the high quality QDs are synthesized at extreme temperature in a reaction medium of high boiling point organic solvents, such as tri-n-octylphosphine oxide (TOPO) and hexadecylamine (HDA). These are long alkyl chain–containing solvents capable of preventing the formation of a bulky structure by coordinating with the unsaturated metal atoms on the QDs. In this process, semiconductor precursors are rapidly injected into suitable organic solvents (hot and vigorously stirred) containing substances capable of coordinating with the surface of the precipitated QD particles. It is also known as the one-pot method, as the methodology is simple. The developed QDs have an organic ligand as a capping layer that facilitates them by only being soluble in nonpolar solvents such as chloroform. The capping process enhances the stability of the QDs and also helps in functionalization or interaction with other molecules. Substances such as thiols and pyrophosphates (mercapto acetic acid, dihydrolipoic acid, trioctyl phosphine (TOP)) are commonly used capping agents. The length and surface density of the capping material are also critical for the stability. Research on QDs is undergoing fruitful transformations to address the major problems, such as formation of larger hydrodynamic sizes (15–30nm), nonspecific cellular bindings, imperfect conjugation chemistry, and reduced brightness.

In this process, components such as QD precursors, organic surfactants and solvents are used (Farkhani and Valizadeh, 2014). Organometallic reagents (volatile metal alkyl, dimethylcadmium) or metallic oxide, CdO, and a chalcogen source (Tri-n-octylphosphine selenide) are rapidly injected into a hot coordinating solvent or hydrophilic-native inorganic ligand, such as TOPO, TOP, and HDA at 300°C. The critical factors, such as nucleation time, pH, and temperature are subjected to standardization to achieve the optimal size of QDs.

This method enables the production of hydrophobic QDs, such as CdS, CdSe, CdTe, with narrow PL properties. The procedure for the synthesis of high quality CdSe QDs is given in Fig. 12.5 (Wong and Stucky, 2001). The reaction process is set up at 300°C to liquefy the hydrophobic capping agents that can complex with the precursors used. QY obtained from this process is higher in percentage (20%–60%), compared to lower percentage (30%) for the aqueous synthesis (Dhar and Deepika, 2014). The QDs prepared from these techniques are not biocompatible and need further solubilization in aqueous media, before usage. Moreover, they are chemically very reactive in the quantum size ranges. These problems can be alleviated by bonding or passivation of surface of QDs with chemically inert materials.

In this method, the reactions are carried out in aqueous media and the QDs synthesized from this method exhibit good biocompatibility and stability. The acetate, nitrate, or chloride of heavy metals are generally used as precursor molecules. Inert atmosphere is maintained inside the reaction vessel as the phosphine-free chalcogenide precursors, such as NaHSe or Na2SeO3, are unstable under normal conditions. Mercapto acids, such as thioglycolic acid (TGA), mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), mercaptosucinnic acid, glutathione (GSH), and l-cysteine (Cys), are some of the commonly used capping molecules. The stearic hinderance posed by these capping materials prevents the agglomeration of nanoparticles. Bao et al. (2012) synthesized CdSe (QY, 10%) and CdSe/CdS QD (QY, 30%) crystals using mercaptoacetic acid as capping ligand and demonstrated its applications in fluorescence imaging of cells and blood capillaries. In another study, Jiang et al. (2015) synthesized the CdSe QDs by adding an alkaline solution of mixture of CDCl2 and TGA into the calcogen precursors at zero degree temperature. Recently, ultrasound-assisted methods have also been employed for the aqueous synthesis of CdTe QDs, CdTe/CdS QDs. Mn-bound zinc sulfide quantum dots (Mn:ZnS QDs) with an average diameter of 2.5nm were developed by Labiadh et al. (2013). These were stabilized by 3-MPA using an aqueous-based nucleation-doping approach. Subsequently, it was coated with a ZnS shell for increased PL property up 10%–18%.

This method is based on the deposition or growing of monocrystalline films in a layer-by-layer fashion. Many of the CSQD structures and QD films produced by this process contain a thin shell of higher bandgap material (e.g., AlGaAs) encapsulating the luminescent semiconductor core (e.g., GaAs). The shell material increases the QY (brightness) and photostability of the core at the surface by quenching nonradiative recombination processes. The growth can be restricted by coating with masking compounds (SiO2) and etching the mask with the required shapes. However, the major limitation of this method is the lower density of the QDs due to mask patterns. The QDs fabricated by this process are mostly used for bioimaging applications.

Lithography is a traditional top-down patterning method where a material is coated with a photosensitive resist layer and a pattern of a latent image is created on a substrate material. Subsequently, the pattern is developed by removing either the exposed areas or the unexposed areas. Depending on the source of energy used, the processes are named optical lithography, X-ray lithography, electron beam lithography, or ion beam lithography. During synthesis, quantum wells are covered with a suitable polymer mask of organic polymer film, further covered by a thin layer of metal, and exposed to an electron or ion beam. Here, a combination of high-resolution electron beam lithography and subsequent etching is performed. The drawbacks include inaccurate spatial resolution, often producing contaminations during processing; formation of defects; nonuniformity of size; poor quality of interface; and even damage to the bulk of the crystal itself.

Alternative approaches for QD synthesis include green methods using natural sources. For example, the leaves of Ocimum sanctum as a carbon source were experimented with as a rapid, cost-effective method to synthesize fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). It exhibited a high aqueous stability and high QY of 9.3%, suited for detection of Pb2+ ions (Kumar et al., 2017). In another study, apple juice was used as a raw material for the preparation of fluorescent CDs, based on a green hydrothermal method (Mehta et al., 2015). Researchers have also attempted to use living microbes, particularly bacteria and biomatrix for driving synthesis processes. Incubation of culture medium of Escherichia coli with inorganic ions, e.g., 1.0×10−3 mol/L cadmium source for 2 days, resulted in synthesis of uniform size of cadmium sulfide (CdS) QDs that had a fluorescence emission peak of 470nm and emitted a blue-green fluorescence under UV light (Yan et al., 2017).

Several instrumentation techniques are being used for measuring the optical characteristics of the prepared QDs. The UV-Visible and PL spectra is recorded by spectroscopy and fluorospectroscopy, respectively. QY is an important feature of QDs and is expressed as relative to a reference compound, rhodamine 6G. The particle size is measured by atomic force microscopy (AFM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, crystallinity by X-ray diffraction, functional groups by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, interaction of QDs with target molecule by fluorescence quenching, CD, and isothermal titration calorimetry, etc.

The functionalized QDS in multifunctional platforms act as bioprobes or biolabels. The QDs are surface engineered by bioconjugating or surface modifying with peptides or markers against specific targets. These bioconjugated QD probes are advantageous, compared to unmodified forms, as they still retain the binding kinetics with negligible steric hindrance problems and function as fluorescent proteins. Multiple functions can be integrated onto the surface of the QDs, including diagnostic agents (radioisotope and magnetic), therapeutic agents (anticancer), etc. Several approaches are being used, including covalent formation, passive adsorption, electrostatic forces, and multivalent chelation (Blanco-Canosa et al., 2014).
•
Physical adsorption: biomolecules such as carbohydrates, proteins, and peptides intrinsically have π bonds and therefore can easily be attached to graphene surface by π–π stacking interactions. Electrostatic interaction is another method where the negatively charged QDs (graphenes) can be attached to the positively charged biomolecules;
•
Covalent modification: this is the most commonly used approach, whereby the primary amino group of proteins (–NH2) can be linked to the carboxylic acid group of QDs (–COOH) via carbodiimide-mediated amide formation in the presence of EDC (1-Ethyl-3-[3-dimethylaminopropyl] carbodiimide) and NHS (N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Quantum Dots in Biopharmaceuticals
ce:section.ce:para: Some of the important applications of QDs include biological imaging of living cells, drug delivery, and as advanced nanosensors for diagnostic purposes. These aspects are briefly discussed in the following section. For more detailed information, readers can refer to some of the comprehensive review article in the literature (Gao et al., 2005; Tian et al., 2017; Valizadeh et al., 2012; Onoshima et al., 2015; Zhou and Coleman, 2016; Shen and Liu, 2016; Tabish and Zhang, 2016).

Early detection and diagnosis of diseases have great practical significance, particularly in deadly diseases such as cancer because they can significantly increase survival rates of the patients. Molecular imaging helps in detection and visualization of disease-related biomarkers, such as of tumors, by noninvasive assessment. The commonly used molecular imaging techniques for the early detection and diagnosis of diseases are fluorescence (FL) imaging, magnetic resonance/spectroscopy (MRI/MRS) imaging, X-ray computed tomography (CT), positron emission tomography (PET), ultrasound, and single photon emission tomography (SPECT).

Every optical technique has its own benefits and limitations. For example, FL imaging has the advantages of providing real-time information with very high sensitivity at the molecular level. Conversely, it has low spatial resolution and poor tissue penetration capability. MRI imaging is a noninvasive technique with a high spatial resolution and 3D imaging capability and therefore can penetrate all types of tissues under examination. Noninvasive fluorescence optical imaging is a highly sensitive and powerful tool in biomedicine for longitudinal and quantitative measurements of changes in tissues and organs. However, it does not provide real-time images at cellular levels, due to longer imaging time and lower sensitivity. Both PET and SPECT uses probes based on radionuclides and are associated with poor resolution and high risk of radiation. Similarly, CT imaging also poses radiation risk, as well as poor sensitivity to soft tissues and therefore restricted from use in some clinical applications. Despite all the advantages, no single-model imaging technique provides comprehensive and reliable data. Therefore, we need to rely on multimodal imaging as an ideal method in medical investigations for all the required accuracy (Arya et al., 2005; Wang et al., 2013a,b,c,d,e,f).

The design of imaging agents is very important for accurate diagnosis of a disease and it should offer advantages of high sensitivity, specificity, and low toxicity, as well as a broad applicability. All the imaging or contrast agents contain three basic components, such as ligands, spacers, and reporters. Ligand molecules, such as peptides and antibodies, specifically recognize and bind to the biomarkers of a disease and are attached to a reporter via spacer molecules. Whereas, the reporter molecules are the paramagnetic substances/radionuclides/fluorophores which help in visualization of the biomarker under various imaging modalities.

In imagining techniques, probes with fluorescence and MRI properties are highly desired to obtain macroscopical anatomical information as well as microscopical optical signals. These are obtained by coating gadolinium on the surface of QDs. For example, gadolinium-coated carbon dots (Gd-CDs) exhibit enhanced blue PL with a QY as high as 69.86%, excellent water solubility, good cell-membrane permeability and negligible cytotoxicity (Yu et al., 2016). They are also used as a contrast agent in dual-imaging techniques of FL and MR. The other magnetic ion–coated QDs include Fe-coated CdTeS QDs, Gd-coated CdTe QDs, Gd-coated CdSe QDs, and GdS-coated CdTe QDs. In a study by Zhang et al. (2014), GdS-coated CdTe QDs were reported to have 5nm particle sizes with 12% QY and good colloidal stability. Furthermore, they conjugated it with folic acid (FA) for the visualization of tumor region in rat (Fig. 12.7 ).

The application of biomedical optics is strongly limited as the photons are scattered and absorbed when in contact with tissue. The penetration capability of the photons depends on the type of tissue being examined, as well as the wavelength (λ) used. The absorption is limited after the 700nm region. This region is referred to as NIR, comprising the first window of 700–950nm range and the second window of 1000–1300nm range. The optical imaging in the NIR region is more promising, as it provides deeper imaging penetration, improved signal-noise ratio, and lower fluorescence background and scattering intensity of NIR light (Martinić et al., 2017). Therefore, QDs absorbing and emitting in the biological diagnostic window of 650–1450nm are useful for imaging purposes. QDs of Ag2S, Ag2Se, Ag2Te, PbS, PbSe, Si, Ge, C, CuInS2, CuInSe2, AgInSe2 are some of the water soluble NIR-emitting particles (Cassette et al., 2013). NIR-QDs are sensitive to change of Cu2+ concentration in the internal environment of cells and the effect can be observed using in vitro and in vivo fluorescent imaging techniques (Tao et al., 2016). Che et al. (2016) developed near-infrared AgInSe2–ZnSe QDs with good water monodispersibility. The QDs showed an PL emission peak ranged from 625 to 940nm, with QYs up to 31%. These crystals had a very low toxicity and were considered as promising candidates for bioimaging.

In breast cancer diagnosis, the presence of metastatic cells is identified by examination of associated auxillary lymph nodes. Particularly, the sentinel lymph node (SLN) is the major one to receive the drainage of primary tumor and is therefore associated with spreading of metastatic cells (Radenkovic et al., 2016). Currently, medical experts perform an SLN biopsy in order to monitor the state of the auxillary lymph nodes. QDs can be used in such cases of cancer patients where mapping of SLN and real-time image-guided resection of tumor is possible. From a study by Hafian et al. (2014), they developed a probe for imaging the carcino-embryonic antigen on normal human appendix, and colon carcinoma tissue by bioconjugating the CdSe/ZnS QDs with 13-kDa single-domain antibodies (sdAbs) derived from camelid IgG. This approach helps in the detection of low concentrations of antigens, using multiphoton microscopy.

The fluorescence imaging of organelles, particularly in regions like the brain, is very challenging, as they pose a physiological challenge for the imaging agent that must cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB). The cellular-uptake of drugs can be also monitored in therapeutic treatments. In a study, a novel nanoprobe of less than 100nm was designed by conjugating biotinylated NGR peptides to avidin-PEG-coated QDs. This could cross the BBB and bind to target, CD13-overexpressing glioma and tumor vasculature. This type of probe provides benefits in fluorescence imaging of brain malignancy (Huang et al., 2016a,b). Justin et al. (2016), synthesized graphene oxide–iron oxide QDs for fluorescent and magnetic resonance dual-modality imaging in dermal fibroblast cells and showed that the drug release can be externally controlled upon magnetic stimulation.

Recently, many drugs have been successfully demonstrated to have been delivered at the target sites using various QDs. The strategy for those drug delivery processes is discussed here. The functionalization of the QDs needs optimization, as some of the resistant cells exhibit resistance at cellular levels, and, therefore, the improvement of chemotherapy efficacy of anticancer drugs is critical for better treatment.

Cisplatin (cis-diamminedichloroplatinum (II), CDDP) is one of the promising chemotherapeutic agents with >90% cure rate used for cancer treatment, especially for testicular cancer. The derivatives of drugs such as cisplatin, oxaliplatin, and carboplatin have received worldwide approval for clinical use. However, the pharmacological effects of platinum complexes suffer from adverse effects and inherent/acquired resistances. However, the use of nanoscale materials (e.g., graphene quantum dots (GQDs)) for the delivery of CDDP has received much attention in the recent past. Sui et al. (2016) developed combinations of GQDs with CDDP that significantly improved the CDDP-induced cytotoxicity, cell cycle arrest, and DNA fragmentation by enhanced cellular uptake of the drug in CDDP resistant cells. The conjugated form of GQDs with cisplastin enhanced the pharmaceutical performance of the drug in terms of cytotoxicity, cell cycle arrest, and DNA fragmentation.

A schematic diagram for the folic acid–based drug delivery is presented in Fig. 12.8 . Recently, magnetofluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based on gallium, nitrogen and sulfur and gadolinium, nitrogen, and sulfur (GdNS@CQDs) have been shown to have excellent fluorescent and magnetic properties. The same can be functionalized in a conjugated form of QDs with folic acid (FA-GdNS@CQDs) for targeting dual modal fluorescence/magnetic resonance (MR) imaging, as confirmed in cell lines of HeLa and HepG2. An anticancer drug (doxorubicin)-incorporated FA-GdNS@CQDs exhibited a high quantity of drug-loading capacity (about 80%) with a pH-sensitive drug release. The uptake and intracellular location of the FA-GdNS@CQDs can be observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy. They also demonstrated in vivo fluorescence bioimaging of the FA-GdNS@CQDs, used in zebrafish as an animal model (Chiu et al., 2016). Similarly, the anticancer efficacy of 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) can be enhanced using a suitable carrier system that targets the folate receptors of the malignant tumor tissues. In an experiment, the 5-FU drug was loaded onto Mn-ZnS QDs encapsulated with chitosan (CS) biopolymer and conjugated with FA. The 5-FU@FACS-Mn:ZnS QDs exhibited selective antitumor effect in MDA-MB231 breast cancer cells in vitro and 4TI breast cancer cells in vivo (Yaghini et al., 2016).

A parenteral multifunctional delivery system, based on nanoparticles (nanostructured lipid carriers) for the delivery of cancer drug was reported recently (Olerile et al., 2017). Here, the QDs (CdTe/CdS/ZnS) and the drugs (paclitaxel) were coloaded by emulsion-evaporation method. The nanoparticle exhibited a biphasic pattern of drug release with growth inhibition rate of 75%. The imaging results were significant in target detection of the H22 tumor.

Theranostic nanomedicines are multifunctional nanomaterials with a combination of imaging and therapeutic agents. Here, simultaneous imaging and targeted delivery of drugs in complex biological systems are possible. They also aid in noninvasive monitoring of the drug delivery process at the targeted tissues. In a recent report, GQDs were prepared from cow milk (Thakur et al., 2016). The anticancer drug, berberine hydrochloride (BHC) was attached to GQDs via cysteamine hydrochloride (Cys). This theranostic GQDs@Cys-BHC complex had 88% drug-loading efficiency. It also showed a potent cytotoxic effect on different cancerous cell line models: cervical cancer cell lines such as HeLa cells and breast cancer cells such as MDA-MB-231.

CQDs are biocompatible, and have high photostability and low toxicity. CQDs developed from alginate, 3% hydrogen peroxide, and double distilled water by one-step hydrothermal carbonization method exhibit stable fluorescence with water-dispersible and positively-charged properties. As this, QDs can complex with DNA to form CQDs/pDNA complexes that can be used as efficient nonviral gene vectors and bioimaging probes in biomedical applications (Zhou et al., 2016).

QDs also act as a promising drug in treating a neurodegenerative disorder such as Alzheimer’s disease (AD). GQDs, when integrated with neuroprotective peptide glycine-proline-glutamate (GQDG) prevent aggregation of Aβ fibrils and thereby reduced the plaque deposits in APP/PS1 transgenic mice. They also further enhanced learning and memory capacity of the mice (Xiao et al., 2016).

QDs as a fluorescent probe can selectively detect and monitor an array of biomolecules and enzyme activities, based on FRET mechanism. The Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), resonance energy transfer (RET) or electronic energy transfer (EET) is a process by which an electronically excited donor fluorophore transfers its near-field energy through nonradiative dipole–dipole coupling to an acceptor fluorophore within close proximity. Here, the spectral overlap occurs when emission spectra of the fluorescent donor is absorbed by fluorescent acceptor nearby (Chou and Dennis, 2015; Stanisavljevic et al., 2015). The FRET occurs when the energy-pairs are in <10nm distances. In the same distance, many bioreactions of the human body, such as DNA hybridization, antibody-based immunological recognition, and enzyme-catalyzed reactions also occurs. Therefore, FRET-based biosensors are widely adopted in the detection of such biological reactions by turn-off/on of fluorescent signals (Fig. 12.9 ).

CQDs and GQDs have received extensive attention due to greater biocompatibility, water solubility, low toxicity, etc., and offer low cost of production. They are also known as carbon nanolights. CQD-based sensors have been utilized for visual monitoring of several biomolecules (Namdari et al., 2017). Table 12.1 shows a range of QDs in biosensing applications, including measurement of biomolecules such as enzymes, vitamins, ions, etc.

An assay for acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity in human serum and seminal plasma was developed using CQDs as the signal reporter (Qian et al., 2016). Acetylcholinesterase enzyme catalyzes the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine into
thiocholine, which induces fluorescence recovery by the interaction of thiocholine with copper ions. Fluorescence quenching occurs through aggregation of CQDs by copper (II) ions. Limit of detection (LOD) was as low as 4.25U/L and detection range was 14.2–121.8U/L. The concentration of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) can be measured by recording the response signal of 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), which is the oxidation product of o-phenylenediamine. In this case, amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs) were employed as the reference fluorophore (Huang et al., 2016a,b). The fluorescence intensity of DAP increases at 553nm, along with a simultaneous quenching of af-GQDs fluorescence at 440nm. This fluorescence intensity ratio of DAP to af-GQDs was linear, up to a certain range of HRP concentrations (ratiometric-fluorescent nanosensor).

β-Glucuronidase (GLU) is one of the important biomarkers for tumor diagnosis. Nitrogen-coated carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with green PL, based on the inner-filter effect (IFE) was reported recently by Lu et al., 2016. The signal of GLU activity can be measured by measuring the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. GLU catalyzes the degradation of 4-nitrophenyl-β-d-glucuronide (PNPG) into product (p-nitrophenol (PNP)). This PNP is absorbed in IFE to turn-off the fluorescence of N-CQDs, due to the complementary overlap between the absorption of PNP and the excitation of N-CQDs. The IFE has no requirement for electron or energy transfer process, nor any chemical modification of fluorophore, compared to other fluorescence sensing mechanisms, such as FRET or PET. N-CQDs had the enzyme activity range of 1–60U/L and with a low detection limit of 0.3U/L. This method helps in detection of clinical tumor diagnosis and antitumor drug screening, based on serum samples analysis.

For the treatment of diabetic conditions, use of α-glucosidase inhibitors is recommended and these are substances intended for inhibition of glucose absorption from the GI tract. Recently, a QDs-based screening of antidiabetic drug has been synthesized. Li et al. (2016a,b, 2017) developed CDs conjugated with cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanoflakes for the screening of acarbose, an inhibitor. α-Glucosidase enzyme catalyze the hydrolysis of l-ascorbic acid-2-O-α-d-glucopyranosyl (AAG) into ascorbic acids (AAs) that further reduces CoOOH nanoflakes into Co2+, which, in turn, causes a fluorescence recovery of CDs by stopping the FRET. The LOD is 5nM. The rate of inhibition of acarbose was in logarithmic proportion to 10−9–10−3 M concentration. The assay can also be used for screening several inhibitors from other natural medicines.

GQDs can be used for the detection of vitamins (e.g., ascorbic acid) and enzymes (e.g., alkaline phosphatase). In this assay, para-benzoquinone (BQ) was used as a quencher of the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. Conversely, if BQ is reduced by ascorbic acid then a fluorescence signal turn-on can be observed (Huang et al., 2016a,b). In an electrocatalytical based assay, Shadjou et al. (2016) designed GQDs with β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) by electrodepositing GQD and β-CD onto the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). This nanocomposite is sensitive to the electrooxidation of vitamin C. Recovery of the GQDs fluorescence occurs when alkaline phosphatase (ALP) hydrolyzes l-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) to produce ascorbic acid. The measured fluorescence intensity was directly proportional to the concentration of AA or ALP in the concentration ranges of 5.0×10−7 to 2.0×10−5 mol/L and 1.0–90U/L, respectively. A detection probe based on CDs was synthesized from sodium alginate via furnace pyrolysis method. The turn-off fluorescence capabilities of several metals were tested on these CDs. Among them, ferric ions (Fe3+) were shown to be the potential quenchers of the fluorescence of CDs. In the optimized condition, the CDs-Fe3+complex specifically acts as detection probe for AA.

Amino acids can be sensed using nanocomposite QDs containing Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles/graphene quantum dots (Fe3O4 MNP-GQDs). Later, the QDs are electrodeposited onto the GCE in the potential range from −1.0 to 1.0V. This setup can capture and enhance the electrochemical signals of some amino acids at varying physiological pHs (Hasanzadeh et al., 2016).

For immunosensing of cancer biomarkers, an array of QDs have been reported; graphene QD, for example (Hasanzadeh and Shadjou, 2017). DNA methyltransferase enzyme can be considered a predictive cancer biomarker when it is abnormally expressed in cells. Early diagnosis of cancer is based on detection of DNA methylation of cancer-related genes, such as PCDHGB6, HOXA9 and RASSF1A in nonsmall-cell lung cancer tissues. QDs-based FRET nanosensors can detect a varying degree of promoter methylation in the early stage of NSCLC tissue samples and also in noninvasive brushing specimens of lungs. The level of methylation can be quantitatively measured by signal amplification during FRET (Ma et al., 2016).

Li et al. (2016a,b, 2017) demonstrated the application of QDs for the detection of tumor cells from human peripheral blood samples. The circulating tumor cell lines (MCF-7 and HepG2 cells) were detected simultaneously in human blood, based on the inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) with gold nanoparticles and QD labeling. In this ICP-MS assay, magnetic beads coupled with anti-EpCAM were used for capturing the cells and labeled by signal probes of CdSe QDs-anti-ASGPR and Au NPs-anti-MUC1. The LOD was in the linear ranges of 200–40000 and 300–30000, respectively, for MCF-7 and HepG2 cells.

Rapid detection of ovotransferrin (OVT) has been reported from egg powder, based on a immunosensor method. Fluorescence signal is turned off when QDs-OVT interacts with anti-OVT-AuNPs by FRET process. However, competitive immunoreactions occur when both the free OVT and QDs-OVT are present in same sample. As a result, recovery of the optical intensity of QDs (turning on) occurs in the OVT concentration of 0.05–3.8μg/mL. The LOD was found to be at 23.55ng/mL (Wang et al., 2016a,b).

GQDs have also been engineered for the determination of neurotransmitters such as dopamine in biological samples (Zhao et al., 2016). For example, the native blue fluorescence of GQDs in aqueous solution is quenched by dopamine, due to electron transfer from the photoexcited GQDs to dopamine. The fluorescence quenching was in the linear range of 0.25–50μM dopamine with LOD of 0.09μM.

Several FRET-based assays have been developed for detecting an array of heavy metal ions such as Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+ that commonly contaminate food, water, and clinical samples. An hexametaphosphate capped CdS QDs can detect calcium ions (Ca2+), based on fluorescence enhancement of CdS QDs induced by Ca2+. The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and Ca2+ was in the range of 10–400μmol/L, with the detection limit of 4μmol/L. The Ca2+-induced fluorescence enhancement of CdS-QDs is inhibited by the presence of fluoride ions, due to formation of calcium fluoride and therefore the same QDs can be used for detection of fluoride ions (Liu et al., 2016a,b).

Kumar et al. (2017) designed fluorescent CDs using green synthesis, where the leaves of O. sanctum was used as a carbon source. These CDs exhibited a greater stability in aqueous solution with the QY of 9.3%. As a unique fluorescent sensor, it can detect Pb2+ ions in water and the CDs emit bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet illumination. It can be used as a fluorescent probe for cellular imaging. CDs can also undergo spontaneous FRET to vitamin B2 (Kundu et al., 2016)

Liu et al. (2016a,b) developed GQDs co-coated with nitrogen and sulfur (N-S/GQDs) that exhibited blue luminescence, with the QY of 36.3%. The N and S in the hybrid interact with Fe3+ and therefore are sensitively quenched by the iron. Therefore, it can be used for quantitative measurement of iron in the serum samples of arthritic patients. The linear recovery of fluorescence of Fe3+-quenched N-S/GQDs occurs when pyrophosphate ion (PPi) is present in the range of 1μM–1000μM concentration.

QDs also used as a novel method of detection of pathogens, based on FRET-based on/off fluorescence. It is also a simple, sensitive, and powerful way of identifying the microorganisms. Graphene oxide (GO) is one of the suitable elements for biological applications, due to its water dispersibility and versatility for surface modification. In a study by Liu et al. (2010), GO sheets–based DNA biosensor was developed for detection of Staphylococcus aureus gene. The amino group of probe DNA (single-strand DNA) was coupled to the carboxyl group of GQD by EDC/NHS cross-linking method and this SS-DNA-GQD was used as donor in the FRET-based assay. The thiol group of complementary or target SSDNA was coupled onto the surface of AuNP and this was used as an acceptor AuNP. The fluorescent emission intensity of GQD is greatly reduced when the SS-DNA-GQD is hybridized with the SSDNA-AuNP, approximately >80% fluorescent quenching (Fig. 12.11 ). Similarly, the MecA sequence of the same organism was detected with the LOD of 1nM for S. aureus gene by Shi et al. (2015).

A sensitive sandwich assay for the detection of E. coli, using immunomagnetic separation and fluorescence techniques, was reported by Dogan et al. (2016). In this, magnetic nanoparticles were of iron oxide core gold shell (Fe3O4@Au), combined with biotinylated antibodies specific to E. coli and chitosan-coated CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) used as the fluorescent label. The fluorescence intensity was linear, in the range of 102–108 cfu/mL; the LOD and limit of quantification values ware at 30 and 100cfu/mL, respectively.

Leishmaniosis is a zoonotic disease caused by Leishmania. This can be detected using a combination of magnetic-bead and cadmium selenite QD probes, selective for Leishmania-specific surface antigens (proteins) and DNA. Magnetic-bead capture probes are used for isolation of the analyze and the target molecule is detected by QD probes. The LOD was 3125ng/μL and 103 cells/mL for Leishmania DNA and protein, respectively (Andreadou et al., 2016). QDs of magnetic cobalt-zinc ferrite (Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4) are used in an electrochemical genosensor where the QDs are used as an electrode modifier to immobilize a 24-mer-specific single stranded DNA probe that binds with the target single stranded DNA (Heli et al., 2016).

For Mycobacterium tuberculosis detection, a sandwich assay, based on antigen-antibody interaction, was developed (Kim et al., 2017). Here, recombinant antibodies bound to the silica-coated QDs and gold nanorods specifically interacted with pathogen-specific secretory antigen, Ag85B causing quenching effects. QDs also used in multiplexed immunosensors for simultaneous detection of E. coli O157: H7 and Salmonella. Compared to conventional QD-based sensor, the QD-RSA-based immunosensor has 50-fold higher sensitivity (Yin et al., 2016).

The detection of clinically isolated influenza virus H3N2 and norovirus-like particles (NoV-LPs) have been successfully demonstrated with immunofluorescence-based nanobiosensors by Takemura et al. (2017). The QDs were made by bioconjugation of antineuraminidase (NA) antibody (anti-NA Ab) to thiolated AuNPs, and the conjugation of antihemagglutinin (HA) antibody (anti-HA Ab) to alloyed quaternary l-cysteine-capped CdSeTeS QDs. These antibodies specifically bind to the antigens on the surface of the influenza virus target, which, in turn, induces a localized surface plasmon resonance signal. Fluorescence-enhancement changes are proportional to the concentration of the target influenza H1N1 virus with the detection limit of 0.03pg/mL in deionized water and 0.4pg/mL in human serum.

The major concern associated with QDs usage in numerous biological applications is the safety aspect. Several studies have reported on the in vivo toxicity of QD using in vitro cell lines (human embryonic kidney cells HEK293, human keratinocytes HaCaT cells, etc.) and variety of animals studied (vertebrates and nonvertebrates). In some experiments, the treatment with QDs showed no obvious toxicity effect, as they were tested under physiologically appropriate doses. For example, administration of CdSe/CdS/ZnS QDs did not cause adverse effect or inflammation in major organs of the primates (Ye et al., 2012). In some cases, the conclusive evidence of toxicity was very difficult to establish. However, multiple experiments have categorically shown that they damage the major organs such as the brain, heart, spleen, lung, and intestines of experimental animals. The general toxicological mechanism attributed to QDs includes excessive generation of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS), depletion of GSH that, in turn, results in damage of biomolecules such as proteins, lipids and DNA, and consequently leads to mitochondrial malfunctions and apoptosis.

The majority of the currently used QDs contain cadmium metal. The International Agency for Research on Cancer classified cadmium as a carcinogenic agent to humans and animals. Some of the QDs of CdSe, CdS, and CdTe pose inherent toxicity, due to heavy metal composition. In animal studies, QDs administration may result in release of free radicals of heavy metals from the QD core into the blood circulation. Various biochemical reactions can also surface-oxidize the QDs, leading to the release of more heavy metal free radicals. Finally, cellular accumulation of toxic heavy metals causes cell death.

QDs are endocytosed by dermal cells and show negligible QD penetration into the epidermis, hair follicles, sebaceous and sweat glands, nerves, and blood vessels. However, further studies are warranted on the retention and migration patterns of interstitial QDs (Rotomskis, 2016). Because of their tiny size, QDs can cross the plasma membrane easily through pinocytosis. The uptake of QDs was mediated via receptors, such as G-protein-coupled receptor and low density lipoprotein receptor in human epidermal keratinocytes, as reported by Zhang and Monteiro-Riviere (2009). In a comparative study, the toxicity of the biosynthesized and microwave-synthesized QDs was evaluated using in vitro cells of human foreskin fibroblast, human prostate cancer cells (PC-3) and breast cancer cells (MCF-7). It was found that the toxicity of biosynthesized QDs to the PC-3 cell lines was ~35% lower, in comparison with the QDs made by radiation method (Kominkova et al., 2017).

The toxicological studies on Cd-containing QDs, core-shell CdS-chitosan nanoconjugates, showed varying degrees of cell viability in human cell cull lines. Intravenous administration of CdS nanoconjugates in BALB/c mouse for 30 days showed no adverse symptoms, but a localized fluorescence was detected in liver tissues of the mice (Carvalho et al., 2017). The effects of cadmium telluride (CdTe) on the male reproduction system have been reported for a period of 90 days recently by Li et al. (2016a,b, 2017). QDs were administered intravenously at the low (0.2nmol) and high dose (2nmol) per mouse. At high dose, the body weight up to 15 days after exposure was affected. The metals were slowly accumulated in the testes and damaged the tissue structure for both doses on day 90 in a dose-dependent manner. However, they did not affect the sperm quantity. The hazardous effects of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) in GC-2 and TM4 cells were studied by Ji et al. (2016). The results confirmed their deleterious nature on the male reproduction by blocking autophagic flux that further reduced the activity of cathepsin B, and inhibited the lysosome proteolytic capacity. In a study by Kim et al. (2016), three forms of GQDs viz. nonfunctionalized, hydroxylated, and carboxylated GQDs at various concentrations (0–1mg/mL) and incubation times (0–4h), were tested on RBC cells. At higher concentration of >750μg/mL, the rate of hemolysis and alterations of rheological properties were observed for the carboxylated form.

Carvalho et al. (2017) studied the cytotoxicity effect of the surface-biofunctionalized CdS QDs (biocompatible aminopolysaccharide shell) in human cell cultures, such as osteosarcoma cell line (SAOS), non-Hodgkin’s B cell lymphoma (Toledo), and human embryonic kidney cell line (HEK293T). At all the concentrations of 10, 50, and 100nM, the cell viability was inhibited in a dose- and time-dependent manner. The in vivo toxicity of noncadmium QDs, such as InP/ZnS QDs, PbSe QDs, PbS QDs, InAs-QDs were not as thoroughly investigated as cadmium QDs. In a report, the long-term effect of InP/ZnS QDs was studied in BALB/c mice by Lin et al. (2015). No obvious side-effects were reported throughout the experimental period of 84 days at the high concentration of 25mg/kg, in terms of change in body weight, hematology, blood biochemistry, or organ histology. Studies also showed that surface passivation of QDs plays a crucial role in lowering cytotoxicity by reducing the breakdown of QDs. Exposure of head-nose of wistar male rats (0.52mgCd/m3) to CdS/Cd(OH)2 QD (6h/day on 5 days) resulted in local neutrophil inflammation in the lungs, indicating short-term inhalation toxicity, but the effect was regressed after 3 weeks of a recovery period (Ma-Hock et al., 2012).

In humans and animals, the chemical tolerance is generally mediated through adenosine triphosphate-binding cassette (ABC) transporters such as P-glycoprotein and multiresistance associated protein 1–5. They are involved in the elimination of adverse chemicals, including QDs. The modulation of cytotoxicity of CdTe QDs in human liver (HepG2) and kidney cells (HK-2) was observed through the induction of ABC transporters and the effect was greater than for CdCl2 at the same concentration. Accordingly, it suggests that the adverse effect results from the combination of free Cd2+and QDs (Chen et al., 2016a,b).

Tang et al. (2013) showed that 24h of CdSO4 or CdTe QD exposure to zebrafish liver cells resulted in decreased cell viability, increased formation of ROS, and an induction of DNA strand breaks. The mechanism included both cadmium-induced toxicity and other routes. In an invertebrate model organism, Caenorhabditis elegans, the cadmium QDs induce complete autophagic process and by this defensive strategy, the organism effectively clears the affected endosomes in intestinal cells (Zhou et al., 2015).

QDs are the emerging nanoparticle-based materials with excellent luminescent properties for various applications. However, biocompatibility of QDs remains subject to explorational study; the development of less toxic QDs needs systematic and thorough scientific investigations. No appreciable toxicity was found in some cases, as experiments fail to predict and correlate the responses of the in vitro/in vivo studies. Therefore, thorough investigations on the safety use of QDs would expand their usage in humans.
